Essentially, I open a second front in the AI war, seeing which model answers first. It’s ridiculous – like hitting the “close door” button on an elevator repeatedly. But it gives me the illusion of control. Worst case, I get a head start from another model; best case, Claude answers and I feel like I set a backup plan in motion. Of course, juggling multiple AIs at once comes with its own mental context-switching cost… but that’s a later realization. In the moment, it feels proactive! I am productive! – I pop open Slack and scroll aimlessly through channels, reacting with emoji here and there, chiming in on random threads (“Haha yes, totally!” to a joke in #random, or “Good point, we should consider that” in a project discussion I half-followed). The goal is to seem present and engaged at work. This is pure because mentally I’m half-absent, my brain still stuck on the problem I asked Claude to solve. I’ll even start drafting status updates: “Working on X, just ironing out Y,” to post later – essentially narrating progress I to make once Claude comes back with results. Slack is both a distraction and a stage during these times. I know I’ve truly spiraled when I start scrolling our company’s #announcements channel from last week for lack of anything better to do.The VS Code Peek-and-Poke – Sometimes I’ll stay in my editor and do ultra-menial clean-up. I’ll comb through code files fixing typos in comments, re-alphabetizing import statements, or adjusting indentation to be perfectly consistent. These are things asked me to do and that probably make zero difference, but it’s code-related busywork that feels satisfying. It’s basically tidying my room while I wait for the genius to call me back. On one occasion, I even started renaming variables to more pleasing names (only to later regret it because Claude’s suggestion used the variable names… ). On the bright side, at least my codebase comes out a tiny bit cleaner after each of Claude’s longer daydreams. – And finally, when the wait is really long and I’m really tired, I descend into a mini existential crisis. It starts innocently: “Hmm, what’s taking Claude so long?” That becomes, “Is Claude… stuck? Did I break it? Do even know what I’m doing?” Which snowballs into, “Why am I even in this field? Am I about to be outdone by a machine? What’s the meaning of a developer’s work in the age of AI?” Next thing I know, I’m questioning the trajectory of my career, the future of humanity with AI, and whether I should have listened to my college roommate and gone into consulting. This is usually when I realize I’m spiraling – fully , as the kids say. The funny thing is, the moment Claude finally responds, all those grandiose worries evaporate as I excitedly read the answer. Crisis postponed, back to coding. (I sometimes imagine Claude purposely delaying just to mess with me: “Let’s see if we can induce a minor identity crisis this time, mwahaha!”).Whew. So those are my – if you can call them that – to cope with Claude’s thinking breaks. Are any of them ideal? Absolutely not. Effective? Debatable (leaning toward for most). Hilarious in hindsight? I sure hope so, otherwise I just admitted to an awful lot of questionable behavior for nothing. But hey, I never claimed to be handling this well – I’m just handling it, period.The High Cost of Constant Context-SwitchingBy now it’s pretty clear that my attempts to “stay productive” while waiting are a bit… scattershot. If you’re thinking, “This sounds like it would totally destroy your focus,” congratulations, you win a prize. 🎉 The truth is, constantly context-switching (even in the name of productivity) has a cost. In fact, researchers have found that the worst interruptions for getting work done aren’t the external ones – they’re the internal, self-inflicted ones(blog.stackblitz.com). Reading that hit me hard: am my own worst enemy whenever I abandon my task to juggle 13 mini-activities during a 60-second pause.It’s the paradox of our times: I fight so hard to preserve my “flow state” from big disruptions, yet I’ll voluntarily shatter it into a million pieces the second I’m left with a moment of idle time. A developer on Reddit perfectly described the dilemma of those short waits: if a build takes ~1 minute, it’s “too long to just stare at the screen, but too short to actually do something” meaningful (reddit.com). That’s exactly the purgatory I find myself in with Claude. One moment of pause is all it takes for me to alt-tab away, and once I’ve broken away, it’s hard to snap back. Often I’ll end up spending 4-5 minutes on a tangent even though Claude replied after 30 seconds. I effectively create a delay for myself than the AI ever did!This isn’t a new lesson, by the way. It’s basically a remix of the age-old programmer wisdom: don’t break a coder’s focus. Even a tiny interruption, like a “quick” 10:30am meeting, can blow up the whole morning’s productivity – there’s a meme that shows a dev’s schedule: nothing gets done before or after that 10:30 “super quick” meeting because the flow is wreckedcheezburger.com. the struggle is real.A single “super-quick meeting” in the middle of the morning can derail a developer’s flow for hourscheezburger.com. Waiting for Claude can have a similar effect on my day – a brief pause that spirals into a longer derailment.When Claude goes on a 30-second thinking spree, I essentially . I initiate the context-switch; Claude merely provided the temptation. And every context-switch has a re-entry cost – the time to remember where I was, reload the mental state of the code, re-focus my brain. Sometimes I handle it okay. Other times I feel like my head is a shattered snowglobe of thoughts, and I’m desperately trying to get all the flakes to settle back on the original scene.It’s humbling (and a bit amusing, if I detach and observe it academically). I have this super-advanced AI at my fingertips, yet my primitive human brain still struggles with . There’s even research on “fragmented thinking” suggesting that too much task-switching is the silent killer of developer productivityblog.stackblitz.comblog.stackblitz.com. You’d think waiting for an AI would be a passive break, but oh no – I manage to turn it into a fragmentation field. I basically throw my concentration out the window at the slightest provocation.Real talk: I probably need to practice just for those short spans, or doing something truly refreshing like standing up and stretching, rather than manically trying to fill every second. But that’s a lesson I’m still learning. For now, the struggle (and the juggle) continues.Confessions of an Impatient AI AddictAt this point, you might think I’m fed up with Claude. That I’m ready to dump my AI companion for a faster model or revert to old-school methods. Let me confess: I’m not. I’m to this thing. For all the grumbling I do during the waits, I still light up like a kid on Christmas when Claude finally delivers a brilliant solution or speeds through a task that would’ve taken me an afternoon. It’s very much a love-hate (or rather love-frustrate) relationship. I vent about the delays, yes, but the second it gives me what I need, all is forgiven and I’m back to singing its praises in the company tech channel.If anything, this whole experience has made me more … and a bit self-deprecating (as if you couldn’t tell). I recognize my own absurdity in this dance. I mean, who else would open a dozen tabs and philosophize about the meaning of work, all because an AI took 60 seconds instead of 6? It’s almost comically human of me: I found a way to be impatient about a miracle. “Hey, this machine is performing intellectual feats for me that were science fiction a couple years ago – why isn’t it faster!?” Truly, we are difficult creatures to satisfy.I sometimes imagine how this reads to someone in another field. A lawyer or a teacher might say, “Wait, you’re complaining that your takes a minute to do a complex task? Seriously?” And I’d have to sheepishly reply, “Yeah… I know, I know.” It’s a classic tech worker problem – a if there ever was one. I’m literally complaining about waiting for answers that would have been impossible to get so quickly a short time ago. This perspective doesn’t escape me. It’s actually healthy to remind myself of it on occasion, as a reality check.Still, when you’re in the thick of it, trying to keep a flow going, even small delays can feel huge. It’s like being in a high-speed car chase and hitting every red light – sure, you’re still covering ground faster than walking, but , those red lights! The emotional rollercoaster is real. And so the coping mechanisms and mental gymnastics continue, for better or worse.At the end of the day, I do have to hand it to Claude: it’s made me confront my productivity habits in a new light. I’ve learned just how prone I am to “productive” procrastination and how easily I can justify any distraction as “keeping the flow going.” I’ve also learned that maybe – just maybe – I need to chill out a bit. Not every ten-second gap needs to be filled. It’s okay to pause, breathe, maybe even stare out the window (what a concept!) instead of compulsively alt-tabbing. Heck, some developers keep an actual book by their desk for compile waits (reddit.com). Perhaps I could channel a bit of that Zen. (Knowing me, I’d probably choose a book about AI and then end up more riled up, but it’s a thought.)So here I am: an anonymous tech worker who could plausibly be at Google (I’ll never confirm or deny 😉), pouring my heart out in a rogue confessional about my Claude addiction and the antics I pull to cope. Writing this has been cathartic – and yes, I fully started it during one of Claude’s slower responses. Talk about meta-productivity! Instead of just twiddling my thumbs, I’ve created a whole blog post. Take , productiveness theater – I turned you into actual production.Embracing the Wait (Or at Least Making Peace with It)I wish I could end this with a grand solution: a revelation that I’ve mastered patience or a hot tip to eliminate Claude’s wait times completely (Anthropic, if you’re reading, I wouldn’t mind that at all). But the truth is, this is likely the new normal – at least for a while. As we push the boundaries of what these AI models can do, we’re also pushing our own boundaries of how we work alongside them. There will be hiccups, slowdowns, moments where the human is left hanging while the silicon brain grinds away. My saga of waiting is just one colorful example.What I can offer is solidarity and a bit of hard-earned perspective. If you find yourself spiraling while waiting for an AI (or frankly, waiting for anything in this high-speed digital world), know that you’re not alone – and that it’s okay to step back and smile at the absurdity of it all. We are, in the grand scheme, ridiculously fortunate to even have these problems. I bitch about Claude, but I wouldn’t give it up; I just need to adapt. Maybe I’ll actually close a few tabs, or allow myself to do for a minute (imagine that). Maybe I’ll treat those waits as tiny mindfulness breaks – the universe forcing me to slow down. ( – I’m not yet, but maybe someday.)In the meantime, I’ll keep opening tabs and writing snarky commentary in my head to entertain myself. I’ll keep finding the humor in my own impatience. Like a true Casey Newton/Kara Swisher spawn, I’ll call out the nonsense (mostly my own) with a wry smile and a clever punchline. And when Claude finally does finish loading that answer, I’ll happily get back to work – until the next wait, when the cycle repeats.So, what to do while I wait for Claude? The answer, it seems, is anything and everything… and occasionally, absolutely nothing. It’s a work in progress, much like the technology itself. And if all else fails, well, I can always write another blog post about it. After all, you’ve just read one – born in a moment of procrastination, but hopefully ending on a note of genuine insight (wrapped in humor). If that isn’t productiveness alchemy, I don’t know what is.Now if you’ll excuse me, Claude just pinged with a response. Time to actually be productive – no theater required. Until the next wait, dear reader… hang in there, and maybe close a couple of those tabs. We got this. 🚀

item.title: Introducing Nightshade: A Tool Helping Artists to Fight Against AI
item.author: The Markup
item.content:encoded: \ Before my career pivoted to writing the words in news stories, I used to draw the illustrations that ran alongside them. As someone who comes from a background in visuals, I’ve been fascinated by the rise of generative AI text-to-image tools like Stable Diffusion, DALL-E, and Midjourney.\ When I learned about how these tools were trained by ingesting literally billions of images from the web, I was surprised to see that some of my own images were part of the training set, included without any compensation to or approval by yours truly.\ I’m far from alone. By and large, many artists are not happy about how their work (and their signature styles) have been turned into prompts that deprive them of control and compensation of their artwork. But now, a team of computer science researchers at the University of Chicago wants to level the playing field and arm artists with the tools they need to fight back against unauthorized use of their work in training new AI models.\ The paper describes a new tool called “Nightshade” which can be used against these powerful image generators. Named after the deadly herb, Nightshade allows anyone to invisibly alter the pixels of an image to “poison” the image . Along with mislabeled metadata, the “poisoning attack” can help generate incorrect results in image generators—such as making the prompt “photo of a dog” generate a photo of a cat.\ I spoke with Shawn Shan, a graduate researcher and lead student author of the paper, titled “Prompt-Specific Poisoning Attacks on Text-to-Image Generative Models.” The paper was covered extensively in the media when it dropped on Arxiv.org late last month. But I wanted to learn more about what Nightshade means for the fight over artists’ rights online, and the potential that the tool could touch off an arms race between creators and the developers of AI image generators, whose voracious appetite for data doesn’t figure to be sated any time soon.The interview was edited for clarity and brevity.\ Can you tell me a little bit about the work that your team has been doing and what led you to build Nightshade?\ We think at this time, there is really kind of a huge power asymmetry between artists or individual creators and big companies, right?\ A big company just takes your data and there’s nothing artists can really do. OK. So, how can we help? If you take my data, that’s fine. I can’t stop that, but I’ll inject a certain type of malicious or crafted data, so you will poison or it will damage your model if you take my data. And we designed it in such a way that it is very hard to separate what is bad data, what is good data from artists’ websites. So this can really give some incentives to both companies and artists just to work together on this thing, right? Rather than just a company taking everything from artists because they can.\ It sounds like all the attacks that you lay out require the attacker to leave poisoned data in the path of the model that’s collecting data. So it’s too late for images that have already been scraped and fed into models, right? And it only works if someone uses Nightshade, posts an image online, and the image gets scraped at some point in the future?\ Can you describe what one singular piece of poisoned data might look like?\ So we discussed two type of attacks – one is just very trivial, just like OK, all I need to do is to post a cat image, change the alt text to “a picture of a dog” and the model—if you have enough of this—it kind of makes sense the model will start associating “dog” with, you know cat images.\ But that’s fairly easy to remove, right? It’s very clear to a human, but also to many machine systems that this is not correct. So we did some work where we tried to make a cat image that looks like both a cat to a human, but to the model, it will think this is actually a dog.\ Your paper describes how Nightshade could be used by artists as a defense against unauthorized use of their images. But it also proposes some fascinating examples of possible uses by companies. One example that you mentioned in the paper is how Nightshade could be used for advertising by manipulating a model to produce pictures of Tesla cars for instance, when somebody types in “luxury cars” as a prompt. And you also suggest an idea that a company like Disney might use this to defend their intellectual property by replacing Disney characters in prompts with generic replacement characters. Has your team considered where this is all headed?\ Yeah, absolutely. There are probably many use cases. But I think perhaps similar to the DRM [digital rights management] case, you know, you can protect copyright, but there are also tons of misuses of safeguarding people’s content using copyright in the past.\ My take on this space is that it’s kind of about the power asymmetry. Right now, artists really have very limited power and anything will just help tremendously, right? There may be some collateral damage or some side effects of a certain company doing things, but what we think is this is worth it, just to give artists a tool to fight back.\ Another take on this is that some of those entertainment companies, perhaps not Disney, but a small or medium-size game company are also very concerned about AI taking their work. So these can probably also help in those cases as well.\ What countermeasures might AI companies deploy to thwart tools like Nightshade?\ We looked at quite a few kinds of detector mechanisms. Even though we’re trying to make the images look the same, there perhaps are ways to tell the difference, and (the companies that develop image generators) of course have tons of people to do this.\ So you know, it’s possible for them to filter them out, say, OK, these are malicious data, let’s not train on them. In some sense, we also win in those cases because they remove the data that we don’t want them to train on, right?\ So that’s also kind of a benefit of that case. But I feel like there may be some ways (companies) can train their model to be robust against attacks like that, but it’s really unclear what they are doing these days, because they don’t really talk too much about it, to see whether this is actually a really big concern to them or, if they have ways to circumvent it.\ But once we deploy, once we start exploring a little bit more, perhaps we’ll see how these companies feel about it.\ That leads me to my next question, which is, we’re seeing large companies like Adobe and Getty release AI tools that come with the reassurance that they have only been trained on licensed imagery. This week OpenAI (creator of ChatGPT and DALL-E 3) announced that it is offering to help pay for any copyright lawsuits that its business tier customers might be subject to resulting from the use of their products. Considering the legal uncertainty, and now the potential for adversarial sabotage with tools like Nightshade, have we seen the last of the large-scale scraping efforts to train AI models on the open web?\ So I think definitely companies are a lot more careful about what they do and what their services do. It’s kind of like we don’t know where are they getting the data at this point? But I was just playing with open.ai yesterday. In their new model, they’re very careful. Like, you’re not going to be able to use any artist’s name to prompt it unless they were born before the 20th century or something, or it is not able to generate any face images of anyone [Open.AI says their latest model will not allow public figures in prompts]. So like there are things they definitely are concerned about it. And of course, it’s because of these lawsuits, because of these concerns.\ So I wouldn’t be surprised if they stopped—perhaps temporarily—scraping these data sets because they just have way too much data probably. But I think longer term, they kind of have to adapt their model, right? Your model can just be stuck in 2023 and at some point you need to learn something new. So I would say they probably will still keep scraping these websites and perhaps a little bit more carefully. But we don’t know at this point.\ Investigative Data Journalist

item.title: Why investing in growth-stage AI startups is getting riskier and more complicated
item.author: Rebecca Bellan
item.content:encoded: AI startups are reaching growth stage much faster. That's exciting, but also risky, because investors might pour millions into a company only to watch it be unseated in a few months.

item.title: Anthropic releases custom AI chatbot for classified spy work
item.author: Benj Edwards
item.content:encoded: On Thursday, Anthropic unveiled specialized AI models designed for US national security customers. The company released "Claude Gov" models that were built in response to direct feedback from government clients to handle operations such as strategic planning, intelligence analysis, and operational support. The custom models reportedly already serve US national security agencies, with access restricted to those working in classified environments.The Claude Gov models differ from Anthropic's consumer and enterprise offerings, also called Claude, in several ways. They reportedly handle classified material, "refuse less" when engaging with classified information, and are customized to handle intelligence and defense documents. The models also feature what Anthropic calls "enhanced proficiency" in languages and dialects critical to national security operations.Anthropic says the new models underwent the same "safety testing" as all Claude models. The company has been pursuing government contracts as it seeks reliable revenue sources, partnering with Palantir and Amazon Web Services in November to sell AI tools to defense customers.

item.title: UK 'Exploring Plan For Digital ID Cards'
item.author: BeauHD
item.content:encoded:
Mirnotoriety shares a report from the Independent: Downing Street is exploring a proposal to introduce digital ID cards for every adult in Britain in a move to tackle the UK's illegal migration crisis, according to reports. The new "BritCard" would be used to check on an individual's right to live and work in Britain, with senior No 10 figures examining the proposal, The Times has reported.
The card, stored on a smartphone, would reportedly be linked to government records and could check entitlements to benefits and monitor welfare fraud. [...] ... it would cost up to 400 million pounds to build the system and around 10 million pounds a year to administer as a free-to-use phone app.

item.title: Anthropic appoints a national security expert to its governing trust
item.author: Kyle Wiggers
item.content:encoded: Anthropic's long-term benefit trust is a governance mechanism that Anthropic claims helps it promote safety over profit, and which has the power to elect some of the company's board of directors.

item.title: FBI: BadBox 2.0 Android Malware Infects Millions of Consumer Devices
item.author: BeauHD
item.content:encoded:
An anonymous reader quotes a report from BleepingComputer: The FBI is warning that the BADBOX 2.0 malware campaign has infected over 1 million home Internet-connected devices, converting consumer electronics into residential proxies that are used for malicious activity. The BADBOX botnet is commonly found on Chinese Android-based smart TVs, streaming boxes, projectors, tablets, and other Internet of Things (IoT) devices. "The BADBOX 2.0 botnet consists of millions of infected devices and maintains numerous backdoors to proxy services that cyber criminal actors exploit by either selling or providing free access to compromised home networks to be used for various criminal activity," warns the FBI.
These devices come preloaded with the BADBOX 2.0 malware botnet or become infected after installing firmware updates and through malicious Android applications that sneak onto Google Play and third-party app stores. "Cyber criminals gain unauthorized access to home networks by either configuring the product with malicious software prior to the users purchase or infecting the device as it downloads required applications that contain backdoors, usually during the set-up process," explains the FBI. "Once these compromised IoT devices are connected to home networks, the infected devices are susceptible to becoming part of the BADBOX 2.0 botnet and residential proxy services4 known to be used for malicious activity."
Once infected, the devices connect to the attacker's command and control (C2) servers, where they receive commands to execute on the compromised devices, such as [routing malicious traffic through residential IPs to obscure cybercriminal activity, performing background ad fraud to generate revenue, and launching credential-stuffing attacks using stolen login data]. Over the years, the malware botnet continued expanding until 2024, when Germany's cybersecurity agency disrupted the botnet in the country by sinkholing the communication between infected devices and the attacker's infrastructure, effectively rendering the malware useless. However, that did not stop the threat actors, with researchers saying they found the malware installed on 192,000 devices a week later. Even more concerning, the malware was found on more mainstream brands, like Yandex TVs and Hisense smartphones. Unfortunately, despite the previous disruption, the botnet continued to grow, with HUMAN's Satori Threat Intelligence stating that over 1 million consumer devices had become infected by March 2025. This new larger botnet is now being called BADBOX 2.0 to indicate a new tracking of the malware campaign. "This scheme impacted more than 1 million consumer devices. Devices connected to the BADBOX 2.0 operation included lower-price-point, 'off brand,' uncertified tablets, connected TV (CTV) boxes, digital projectors, and more," explains HUMAN.
"The infected devices are Android Open Source Project devices, not Android TV OS devices or Play Protect certified Android devices. All of these devices are manufactured in mainland China and shipped globally; indeed, HUMAN observed BADBOX 2.0-associated traffic from 222 countries and territories worldwide."

item.title: AI startups continue fueling San Francisco’s office recovery
item.author: Connie Loizos
item.content:encoded: Early-stage AI startups are imbuing new life into San Francisco’s Northern Waterfront, after years of "for lease" signs dotting the post-pandemic landscape.

item.title: Tesla’s Optimus robot VP is leaving the company
item.author: Sean O'Kane
item.content:encoded: Milan Kovac, head of Tesla's Optimus humanoid robot program, is leaving the company to spend more time with family, and he said that his support for Musk and Tesla is "ironclad."

item.title: Italian lawmakers say Italy used spyware to target phones of immigration activists, but not against journalist
item.author: Lorenzo Franceschi-Bicchierai
item.content:encoded: A parliamentary investigation answered some — but not all — the questions related to a spyware scandal involving the use of the Israeli company’s spyware, Graphite.

item.title: Stop Guessing Your Brand Strategy—AI Already Knows What Works
item.author: Josh Weaver
item.content:encoded: Brand positioning used to take 18 months, three research phases, and a budget that could fund a small country's space program. Now? AI can surface the insights that actually move metrics in weeks, not fiscal quarters. But here's the plot twist nobody's discussing: most startups are using AI to make the same tired mistakes faster.The real opportunity isn't automating your way to another "innovative solutions for modern businesses" tagline. (Please, for the love of all that's holy, stop.) It's using AI to find the psychological pressure points that actually change behavior. The difference between someone scrolling past your brand and someone converting at 2 AM when they genuinely need you.Think Netflix's algorithm doesn't just recommend shows—it predicts what you'll binge when you're stressed, bored, or avoiding real life. That's positioning with psychological precision, creating brand value through predictive analytics that feels like magic to users.Traditional brand frameworks crumble when stakes get real. I learned this the hard way managing campaigns where success wasn't just market share—it was literal life and death. When you're crafting messaging that needs to break through not just during someone's Instagram scroll, but during their 2 AM crisis moment, focus groups become about as useful as a chocolate teapot.Here's what they don't teach in marketing school: there's a universe of difference between brand awareness and brand recall. Between knowing you exist and thinking of you first when everything's falling apart. Traditional research captures the first. AI can map the second through sophisticated customer segmentation and behavioral analysis.A brand positioning framework should connect your promise to customer needs, but AI lets you map those connections at neural pathway levels. Instead of hoping your message resonates, you can predict which psychological triggers drive action across different target audiences. Instead of guessing what differentiates you, AI surfaces counter-intuitive positioning opportunities hiding in behavioral data that would take human analysts months to uncover.That's why I was grateful to have a discerning team at Qualtrics that really got under the hood of what we needed to know—how do we not only make LGBTQ+ young people aware of us, but how do we deliver that all-important recall during a real time of crisis. Modern AI tools offer cost-effective strategies for entrepreneurs, but the real value isn't replacing expensive consultants. It's compressing the feedback loop between strategic insight and market validation from "someday maybe" to "next Tuesday."Importance of Brand Positioning for StartupsBrand positioning isn't about differentiation—it's about findability. When someone has a problem you solve, can they think of you first? When they're ready to buy, are you already living rent-free in their consideration set? This is where developing a solid positioning strategy becomes crucial for sustainable business growth.Most startups burn through runway chasing everyone instead of being indispensable to someone. It's like dating apps—everyone wants to be "the one" for everybody and ends up being forgettable to anyone. Effective positioning focuses your limited resources on customer segments most likely to convert, not just double-tap your content.The brands that survive economic downturns aren't just memorable—they're necessary. They solve problems people actually have, not problems that sound impressive in pitch decks. A well-crafted positioning statement guides every strategic decision from product development to partnership strategy, ensuring you're building something people will pay for, not just talk about at dinner parties.For startups especially, clear positioning helps achieve product-market fit faster by defining exactly who your ideal customer is and why they should choose you over doing nothing at all. Because let's be honest—inaction is your biggest competitor, not the other startup down the hall.Role of AI in Modern Brand StrategyAI transforms brand strategy from reactive to predictive. Instead of responding to market shifts after TechCrunch writes about them, you can spot them emerging from social listening data. Instead of personalizing at segment level like it's 2015, you can customize at individual level. Instead of A/B testing two sad options, you can optimize across hundreds of variables simultaneously.But here's the thing: predictive analytics, generative AI, sentiment analysis, and natural language processing aren't just tools—they're strategic advantages that separate brands that scale from brands that stall. They let you understand consumer behavior patterns that human analysis misses, identify market trends before they hit mainstream consciousness, and create personalized customer experiences at scale without losing brand coherence.The emergence of vertical AI products has revolutionized how we approach content strategy and marketing strategy performance. AI can now analyze customer feedback in real-time, optimize content performance across platforms, and deliver immersive brand experiences that adapt to individual user preferences. It's like having a marketing team that never sleeps and gets smarter with every interaction.Companies like Farfetch increased email marketing campaigns effectiveness by 40% not through better subject lines, but by using AI to test content styles and tones that matched individual customer communication preferences. That's the difference between broadcasting into the void and actually connecting with humans through data-driven personalization.Understanding Brand PositioningHere's what nobody tells you about positioning: the best insights often contradict conventional wisdom. Back in 2017, I convinced Chanel——to abandon their WWD-first strategy for Snapchat verticals. The data wasn't obvious; it was buried under layers of "that's not how luxury brands behave."We had to work with Facebook (back when they were still pretending to be the good guys) to prove that Millennial women wanted snackable content, not high-gloss editorial spreads. This is a brand whose marketing budget prioritizes the first ten pages of Women's Wear Daily, not some upstart social platform. That 23% brand lift came from challenging a luxury brand's comfort zone with data they couldn't ignore.Today's AI tools could surface those insights in hours, not months of convincing skeptical executives. They can identify when established brand behaviors are actually limiting growth, when customer preferences have shifted before competitors notice, and when contrarian positioning strategies will outperform safe choices. Modern systems can process customer journey data and audience engagement metrics to reveal positioning opportunities that traditional market research would miss entirely.A defensible brand isn't just different—it's indispensable. Especially for AI startups facing consumer skepticism about technology they don't understand and frankly don't trust. Your customer value proposition needs to communicate stability, clarity, and human benefit, not just technical wizardry that sounds impressive to other engineers.Key Elements of Brand PositioningBrand positioning frameworks articulate how you market to your target market by connecting their actual needs with your authentic capabilities. Not what you want to sell because it's technically impressive, but what they actually need to buy because it solves real problems in their daily lives.The frameworks that work incorporate specific images, characteristics, and phrases that differentiate your offering from competitors in ways that matter to potential customers. Generic language gets ignored faster than a LinkedIn influencer's motivational post. Specific language gets remembered, shared, and most importantly, acted upon.Understanding your competitors matters, but understanding your customers' decision-making process matters more. What information do they need? What concerns keep them awake? What would make them choose you over doing nothing at all? This is where customer understanding becomes the foundation of effective positioning strategy.The most crucial aspect isn't establishing your position—it's maintaining it under pressure. When funding gets tight, when competitors copy your messaging, when market conditions shift faster than your ability to pivot, does your positioning hold or does it crumble like a stale cookie?Benefits of a Strong Brand PositionStrong positioning clarifies your target audiences and differentiates you from competitors, but the real benefit is strategic focus. It becomes your North Star for saying no to shiny opportunities that look good on paper but would dilute your impact and confuse your customer segment.Consistent positioning aligns messaging, design choices, and customer experiences across every touchpoint. Your brand becomes instantly recognizable not just visually, but emotionally. People know what to expect from you, which builds the kind of trust that turns browsers into buyers and drives sustainable business growth.When I led Trevor Project's positioning during our rebrand, differentiation wasn't about being different—it was about being findable in the exact moment someone needed us most. That's the difference between brand positioning as academic exercise and brand positioning as survival strategy. Our positioning statement became the foundation for creating brand value that literally saved lives.Strong positioning also improves marketing efficiencies by focusing your efforts on the channels and messages most likely to resonate with your ideal customer. Instead of spray-and-pray marketing campaigns, you can invest in targeted strategies that deliver measurable results and improve customer loyalty over time.AI Integration in Brand StrategyThe American Dreams campaign taught me that changing minds isn't about facts—it's about helping people see themselves as heroes of their own stories. We weren't targeting people eager to support LGBTQ+ rights; we were reaching those actively disinterested. The breakthrough came when we created positive cognitive dissonance, helping them align actions with stated values through storytelling that made them the protagonist.AI's pattern recognition could identify these psychological leverage points across massive datasets. Instead of guessing which values resonate with different audiences, AI can map value systems and predict which messaging approaches will create the cognitive shifts that drive behavior change. It's like having a psychology PhD combined with a supercomputer, minus the student loans.Modern AI integration enables brands to move beyond traditional customer segmentation to create truly personalized experiences at scale. The technology can analyze customer behavior patterns, predict future needs, and deliver content that feels personally crafted for each individual interaction. This level of personalization was impossible just a few years ago but is now becoming table stakes for competitive markets.AI-Driven Market ResearchAI streamlines market research by automating data analysis and customer segmentation, but the real advantage is speed and scale. You can understand target demographics, identify emerging needs, and validate business ideas in weeks instead of waiting for quarterly insights that arrive after your competitors have already moved.Traditional market research asks people what they think they want, which is about as reliable as asking someone to predict their Spotify Wrapped in January. AI analyzes what people actually do—their behavioral patterns, purchase decisions, and engagement data that reveal true preferences versus stated ones.The most sophisticated AI systems can now process social media posts, customer service interactions, and purchasing behavior to create detailed customer profiles that would have required teams of analysts and months of work using traditional methods. This capability is particularly valuable for startups with limited resources who need actionable insights quickly.By automating research processes, AI frees marketing teams to focus on strategic application of insights instead of drowning in data collection. You can leap from "What do customers want?" to "How do we deliver it better than anyone else?" in the time it used to take just to design a survey.AI analyzes market data to identify positioning opportunities your competitors haven't recognized yet. It finds the gaps between what customers need and what's currently available, then suggests differentiation strategies that create actual competitive advantages, not just marketing fluff.Social listening powered by AI reveals not just what people say about brands, but how they feel about them across different platforms and contexts. That emotional data informs positioning strategies that connect with customers on levels beyond rational feature comparisons. Because let's be honest—most purchase decisions are emotional, then rationalized afterward.Advanced analytics can now track marketing strategy performance across competitors in real-time, identifying which approaches drive customer engagement versus mere awareness. This intelligence helps startups position themselves in market gaps while avoiding the costly mistake of competing head-to-head with established players in their strongest positions.AI-driven competitive analysis isn't just about monitoring what competitors do—it's about predicting what they'll do next. Understanding their positioning strategies, identifying their blind spots, and positioning yourself where they can't easily follow without abandoning their core brand identity.AI in Content Creation and PersonalizationAI-driven content creation enables scalable personalization that feels authentic, not automated. Instead of one-size-fits-all messaging that fits nobody perfectly, you can generate brand-aligned content that resonates with specific audience segments across multiple platforms simultaneously.The technology has evolved beyond simple blog posts and social media posts to create immersive brand experiences that adapt to individual user preferences and behaviors. Modern AI can maintain a consistent brand voice while adjusting tone, complexity, and messaging focus based on where customers are in their journey.Personalizing customer experiences with AI means customizing recommendations, communications, and support based on individual behavior patterns and preferences. It's the difference between treating customers like segments on a spreadsheet and treating them like actual people with unique needs, preferences, and pet peeves.The key isn't replacing human creativity—it's amplifying it like a really good sound system. AI can generate hundreds of content variations, but humans decide which ones capture your brand essence and connect with your audience's emotional reality. Technology provides the options; humans provide the soul.Practical Steps for AI ImplementationAfter raising $60MM through strategic brand partnerships, I learned that implementation without measurement is just expensive hope dressed up in fancy dashboards. You need clear metrics, defined success criteria, and feedback loops that tell you when something's working versus when it just feels like progress.AI implementation starts with selecting technologies that match your actual needs, not your aspirational ones. Open-source frameworks for startups with technical teams, no-code platforms for those who think CSS stands for "Can't Style Stuff," cloud services for scalable solutions. The tool matters less than your ability to use it strategically.Training marketing teams on AI outputs is crucial because the technology is only as good as your team's ability to interpret and act on insights. AI generates data, but humans make strategic decisions based on that data. And humans, unlike algorithms, can recognize when something technically correct is strategically stupid.The modern market template for AI tools spans everything from content creation to predictive analytics, but not all tools are created equal. Some specialize in customer relationship management, others excel at analyzing customer preferences and predicting market dynamics. The key is matching specific tools to your unique business model and growth objectives.BrandBuildr.ai offers simplified brand strategy development with 15+ AI-powered models, while Brand.ai provides a comprehensive brand operating system that analyzes business data and market trends throughout your brand lifecycle. For content optimization, Surfer SEO powers 150,000+ customers including Shopify and FedEx with AI-driven SERP analysis.Bynder, named a leader in Gartner's 2025 Magic Quadrant for DAM, uses AI for natural language search and automated tagging—Lucid Motors reduced admin work by 70% and Siemens Healthineers saved $3.5M through their platform. Jasper AI delivers purpose-built generative AI with brand voice training across 90+ marketing apps, while Kompas AI provides advanced conversational AI for deep research and report generation.AI tools should track performance metrics that actually matter—audience engagement that leads to conversion, content effectiveness that drives action, campaign reach that generates measurable results. Vanity metrics are still vanity metrics, even when AI generates them with impressive-looking graphs.Developing a Unique Brand Identity with AIAI automates content creation, but brand identity requires human intelligence about what makes you valuable to customers. Technology can generate infinite options, but you decide which options reflect your authentic value proposition and won't make you cringe in six months.Creating brand value through AI involves using the technology to amplify your unique strengths rather than homogenize your message. The best AI implementations help brands develop deeper insights into what makes them special, then scale those insights across all customer touchpoints.Modern AI can analyze successful brand positioning across your industry to identify patterns and opportunities, but the most effective identities come from understanding your customers' world, not just showcasing your product features. Design should solve problems, not just look pretty in your portfolio.The goal is using AI to create immersive brand experiences that feel authentically you, scaled across every interaction. Whether it's personalized product recommendations or customized communication styles, the technology should make your brand feel more human, not less.Optimizing Brand Strategies Through AI InsightsAI tools analyze audience engagement and campaign effectiveness, providing real-time insights that optimize brand strategies instead of waiting for quarterly reviews that arrive after your budget's already spent. You can identify what's working and double down, or spot what's failing and pivot quickly.Advanced language models can now analyze customer conversations across multiple channels to identify emerging needs, shifting preferences, and opportunities for deeper customer relationships. This level of analysis happens at individual level scale, providing insights that would be impossible to gather through traditional research methods.The most sophisticated systems can now predict customer behavior with startling accuracy, enabling brands to anticipate needs rather than just respond to them. This predictive capability transforms marketing from reactive to proactive, creating competitive advantages that compound over time.Monitoring brand awareness before and after AI implementation reveals whether technology is actually enhancing your strategy or just making you more efficiently mediocre. Because automation without improvement is just organized chaos with better reporting.Maintaining Brand ConsistencyLeading multi-city teams at Vice taught me how brand consistency breaks down across markets—different cultural contexts, different competitive landscapes, different customer expectations. AI could maintain coherence while allowing local adaptation, ensuring your brand remains recognizable while staying relevant to specific contexts.AI analyzes brand attributes to ensure consistency in colors, imagery, and voice across channels, but consistency isn't uniformity. The goal is coherent expression of your brand values, not robotic repetition of the same messages until everyone gets sick of hearing them.Maintaining a consistent brand voice across platforms while personalizing individual customer experiences requires sophisticated balancing. AI can help by establishing core brand parameters that remain constant while allowing tactical flexibility in how those parameters are expressed across different contexts and customer segments.Ensuring Consistent Brand MessagingConsistent messaging across channels is crucial for brand visibility and awareness, but maintaining consistency while personalizing experiences requires strategic balance. AI helps by analyzing brand elements and replicating them across touchpoints while adapting tone and context appropriately.The challenge isn't choosing between consistency and personalization—it's achieving both simultaneously without losing your mind or your brand identity. Your voice should remain recognizable while adapting to different customer needs and communication preferences across the entire customer journey.Modern AI systems can maintain brand guidelines while optimizing message delivery for different audience segments, ensuring your core value proposition comes through clearly regardless of channel or context. This capability is particularly valuable for startups that need to maximize impact across limited marketing channels.Regular Updates and AI Strategy AdaptationsAI-driven strategies adapt continuously by leveraging predictive analytics to forecast consumer trends and behaviors before they become obvious to competitors reading the same industry reports. The advantage isn't just being responsive—it's being anticipatory in ways that feel magical to customers.Testing different content styles and messages becomes efficient with AI, allowing rapid optimization of customer engagement without lengthy campaign cycles that drag on longer than a Marvel movie franchise. You can identify what resonates and scale it quickly across all customer touchpoints.Successful AI strategies evolve with changing market conditions, technology capabilities, and customer expectations. The frameworks that work are flexible enough to incorporate new information without losing strategic focus or requiring complete rebuilds every six months.Case Studies of Successful AI AdoptionMcKinsey's 2018 analysis highlighted AI's marketing potential through analysis of 400+ use cases, identifying $2.6 trillion potential value in marketing and sales applications. But real-world application reveals more nuanced results than consultant slide decks suggest.Between early 2018 and mid-2019, AI implementation in marketing jumped 27%, according to the American Marketing Association. But adoption doesn't equal effectiveness. The brands winning with AI aren't just using more technology—they're using it more strategically, like a chess master versus someone just moving pieces around the board.Harvard Business School research shows that the most successful AI implementations focus on enhancing human decision-making rather than replacing it. Companies that treat AI as a tool for amplifying human intelligence consistently outperform those that view it as a replacement for human insight and creativity.Real-World Examples of AI in Brand StrategyNike's AI-powered fitness ecosystem personalizes workout recommendations through their Run Club and Training Club apps, achieving 3x higher retention compared to guest customers. But the success comes from understanding that customers want guidance, not just information overload. The AI serves human needs instead of showcasing technical capabilities that only impress other engineers.Brands across industries use AI to analyze consumer behavior and market trends for tailored messaging, but the most effective applications focus on emotional resonance, not just behavioral prediction. Understanding what customers do matters less than understanding why they do it—and how you can make their lives genuinely better.Lessons Learned from Startup SuccessesEffective positioning clarifies target audiences and differentiates from competitors, but AI enhances this by identifying positioning opportunities that human analysis might miss. The technology accelerates insight discovery, but strategic application still requires human judgment and cultural intelligence.AI aids in understanding customer data for tailored marketing content, accelerating content creation and storytelling, but effectiveness relies on high-quality data, mindful integration with human insights, and careful attention to cultural nuances that algorithms often miss spectacularly.Startups in competitive fields must develop defensible brands that communicate stability and clarity to overcome consumer skepticism. AI can help identify these positioning opportunities, but building trust with wary customers requires human understanding of their concerns, motivations, and the emotional barriers preventing purchase.Incorporating AI requires clearly defined strategy with technology serving as a supportive tool, not a replacement for strategic thinking. The most successful implementations start with clear goals and use AI to achieve them more effectively, not just more impressively.Privacy concerns demand data collection systems compliant with regulations, but transparency about AI usage also builds trust with customers who want to understand how their data creates value for them, not just profit for you. This transparency becomes a competitive advantage as consumers become more sophisticated about data usage.Human oversight ensures AI aligns with brand objectives and handles unforeseen errors or biases that could damage your reputation faster than a viral TikTok scandal. Technology should amplify human judgment, not automate it into algorithmic obsolescence.Addressing Data Privacy ConcernsCompliance with data privacy laws like GDPR and CCPA is essential for navigating tightening AI regulations, but transparency goes beyond legal requirements. Customers want to understand how AI improves their experience, not just how it serves your quarterly metrics and business objectives.Ethical AI usage mitigates bias and fosters responsible practices, but it also builds competitive advantage. Brands that use AI transparently and ethically will earn customer trust that becomes harder for competitors to replicate through discounts and flashy marketing campaigns.The new EU AI Act establishes comprehensive AI regulation with implementation beginning February 2025, while ISO/IEC 42001:2023 provides the world's first AI management system standard. Data quality checks and risk controls maintain privacy standards while ensuring AI generates reliable insights.Enhancing Human-AI CollaborationAI automates content creation, analyzes trends, and personalizes customer experiences, but strategic and creative direction remains essentially human territory. The goal isn't replacing human insight—it's amplifying it with better information and faster processing power.Practical AI applications in branding include market research and competitive analysis that improve efficiency and provide resources to refine human-generated ideas. The technology should make marketing teams smarter, not replace their thinking with algorithmic assumptions.The most effective human-AI collaboration recognizes what each does best. AI processes data and identifies patterns at scale that would take humans years to discover. Humans provide context, make strategic decisions, and ensure technology serves authentic human needs instead of just optimizing for metrics that look good in reports.Future-Proofing Your BrandAI enhances brand positioning by automating content creation, analyzing trends, and personalizing customer experiences, but future-proofing requires balancing AI capabilities with human strategic direction. Technology should serve your brand essence, not define it through algorithmic convenience.Advanced AI tools assist in creating tailored positioning plans that align with target audience preferences, but the most sustainable brands maintain authentic values while adapting their expression to changing market conditions. Flexibility without foundation leads to brand identity crisis.The balance between AI automation and human strategic direction is crucial for maintaining brand essence while leveraging technology for efficiency and deeper insights. Your brand should feel more human because of AI, not less human despite it.Trends in AI and Brand PositioningAI enables brands to embrace automation and predictive models for streamlined content creation and efficient campaign management, but the most successful applications focus on serving customer needs more effectively, not just operating more efficiently for internal convenience.The emergence of vertical AI solutions creates opportunities for hyper-specialized positioning that wouldn't have been possible with general-purpose tools. Brands can now create immersive experiences tailored to specific industries, customer types, or use cases with unprecedented precision.Implementing AI optimizes technical infrastructure while ensuring experiences remain impactful and compliant with privacy regulations. The goal is sustainable competitive advantage through better customer understanding, not just technological sophistication that impresses investors but confuses customers.AI equips startups with structured yet flexible frameworks that enhance ability to adapt to market dynamics effectively. The technology should make you more responsive to customer needs, not more reactive to competitor moves in an endless game of strategic follow-the-leader.Utilizing AI in brand strategy helps identify profitable marketing channels and optimize budget allocations, but success requires understanding why certain approaches work, not just which ones generate results that look good in monthly reports to stakeholders.As digital marketplaces evolve faster than fashion trends, AI empowers brands to convert complex market data into actionable insights for refined decision-making. The advantage goes to brands that can act on insights quickly while maintaining strategic focus on what actually matters to customers, not just what generates impressive analytics.The most defensible brand position isn't the cleverest or most creative—it's the most necessary. AI can help you find that position faster and defend it more effectively, but only if you remember that technology serves strategy, not the other way around. Because at the end of the day, customers don't buy from algorithms—they buy from brands that understand them, serve them, and make their lives genuinely better.

item.title: Why Hackers ❤️ Your Expired Domains and Outdated JavaScript
item.author: Simon Wijckmans
item.content:encoded: Most companies obsess over phishing emails, firewalls, and endpoint protection but miss a quieter, equally dangerous threat. Do companies know how their web applications behave in the browser of their users? Outdated third-party scripts aren’t just a lapse in IT hygiene. They’re an open invitation to data theft, fraud, and regulatory fallout. When a forgotten domain becomes an attack vectorIn the infamous British Airways breach, attackers registered a lookalike domain (baways dot com) and exploited a third-party script on the airline’s legitimate site to siphon off customer credit card data. For 16 days, the attacker quietly rerouted personal information from britishairways.com (real site) to the fake site. By the time anyone noticed, up to half a million customers had been compromised. The airline faced record-breaking fines and reputational damage.Even more remarkable: our company was recently able to purchase baways.com again. It had been abandoned. We’ve since secured it and now host a history of the breach to raise awareness. Had modern browser supply chain security tools been in place (capable of flagging that baways.com was hosted in Romania by a discount Lithuanian provider, not in the UK) the breach might have been prevented.This isn’t ancient history. The same basic attack vector is still available to cybercriminals. Many organizations simply don’t know which domains they’ve let expire, or what those domains are still connected to.Abandoned domains don’t stay quietA few months ago, Belgian cybersecurity researcher Inti De Ceukelaire conducted a bold experiment. He purchased more than 100 expired domains that once belonged to hospitals, courts, and police agencies. Within days, he had access to 848 email inboxes. Password reset links flowed in for everything from Google Drive to Dropbox to OneDrive.Even more alarming, those dormant inboxes began receiving new messages with folks’ personal health data, court records, and internal police communications. Years after these domains were decommissioned, they were still wired into critical systems and handing over sensitive information for less than €10 per domain.This is what happens when organizations fail to fully sever old connections. Expired doesn’t mean erased, it just means vulnerable. Domain confusion undermines brand trust and securityEven active domains can create security problems when managed poorly. Take the UK’s Royal Mail, which sends users package tracking links using the domain ryml.me followed by randomized strings. With scam texts mimicking this exact format, users can’t reliably tell what’s legitimate. In some cases, Royal Mail’s own support staff have misidentified their own links as fraudulent. That kind of confusion chips away at customer trust and makes phishing easier.Or consider HubSpot, which uses a wide range of domain names (hubspot.com, hs-scripts.com, hsforms.com, hubapi.com, and others) for different services. If a business allows a script to connect to a domain like hs-hubapi dot net (not an actual known domain, but plausible enough), it might inadvertently run malicious code. Conversely, a legitimate script might be flagged as risky if the security team isn’t sure what domains are sanctioned. When domain management lacks clarity, both the security team and the end users are left guessing.The Polyfill breach shows what happens when forgotten code strikes backThe recent Polyfill incident drove this issue home. Attackers bought the domain Polyfill dot io, previously linked to an open source library used across thousands of websites. The project had fallen out of maintenance, but the script was still actively referenced by over 380,000 hosts, including Warner Bros and Mercedes-Benz. Once compromised, Polyfill dot io began injecting malicious code into every page that called it. The breach didn’t stem from a flashy new exploit. It came from a forgotten link buried in a script tag.What organizations must do nowDomain and browser script management are often treated as peripheral concerns. They’re not. The risks are real, active, and growing; regulators are taking notice. PCI DSS 4.0, for instance, now requires (as of March 2025) all organizations handling payment data to implement browser script monitoring.To stay ahead of both attackers and auditors, organizations need to rethink how they manage domain and script risk. That all starts with retaining ownership of all domains tied to internal systems, user accounts, or hosted data (including long after a rebrand or business shift). It also means proactively acquiring lookalike domains to prevent impersonation and typo-squatting attacks before they can begin. Most critically, companies should put in place browser-based supply chain security strategies that can monitor, validate, and block suspicious third-party scripts in real time as pages load.The modern browser is one of the most targeted surfaces in cybersecurity. With so much depending on a few invisible lines of JavaScript or a forgotten domain, security teams can’t afford to look away.

item.content:encoded: The Trump-Musk feud has been great for X, which jumped up the App Store charts.

item.title: Chinese Student Enrollment in US Universities Continues Multi-Year Decline
item.author: msmash
item.content:encoded:
Chinese student enrollment at American universities has dropped to 277,000 in the 2023-24 academic year, down from a peak of 372,000 in 2019-20, according to data in a new report examining shifting global education patterns. The decline accelerated following the State Department's May 28th announcement of an "aggressive" campaign to revoke visas for Chinese students in "critical fields" of science and engineering, as well as those with unspecified Communist Party "connections."
The trend reflects broader economic and geopolitical pressures beyond visa restrictions. Chinese families increasingly view American education as too expensive amid China's economic downturn and property market decline, while domestic employers have grown suspicious of foreign-educated graduates. Meanwhile, Chinese students are choosing alternatives including Britain, which hosted nearly 150,000 Chinese students in 2023-24, and regional destinations like Japan, where Chinese enrollment increased to 115,000 in 2023 from under 100,000 in 2019.

item.title: Figure AI CEO skips live demo, sidesteps BMW deal questions onstage at tech conference
item.author: Marina Temkin
item.content:encoded: Figure AI has drawn attention for making claims that its AI-powered robots possess human-like fine motor skills and can manipulate objects with precision but hasn't done a live demonstration of the humanoids.

item.title: Millions of low-cost Android devices turn home networks into crime platforms
item.author: Dan Goodin
item.content:encoded: Millions of low-cost devices for media streaming, in-vehicle entertainment, and video projection are infected with malware that turns consumer networks into platforms for distributing malware, concealing nefarious communications, and performing other illicit activities, the FBI has warned.The malware infecting these devices, known as BadBox, is based on Triada, a malware strain discovered in 2016 by Kaspersky Lab, which called it "one of the most advanced mobile Trojans" the security firm's analysts had ever encountered. It employed an impressive kit of tools, including rooting exploits that bypassed security protections built into Android and functions for modifying the Android OS's all-powerful Zygote process. Google eventually updated Android to block the methods Triada used to infect devices.A year later, Triada returned, only this time, devices came pre-infected before they reached consumers’ hands. In 2019, Google confirmed that the supply-chain attack affected thousands of devices and that the company had once again taken measures to thwart it.

item.title: Jony Ive’s LoveFrom helped design Rivian’s first electric bike

item.content:encoded: LoveFrom, the creative firm founded by former Apple chief designer Jony Ive, played a role in the development of Rivian’s first electric bike, according to multiple sources who spoke to TechCrunch.
